title: Evidence for a conserved quantity in human mobility
data_sources: Copenhagen Networks Study (CNS) smartphone GPS + WiFi

data_scope.spatial_precision: 50 m (CNS, 95th pct); <100 m (Lifelog, 93rd pct); cell-tower ID (MDC, RM)

core_conclusion: Across ~40,000 individuals from 4 datasets spanning 9-24 months, the number of familiar locations each person regularly visits is a conserved quantity of approximately 25, stable over time even as individual activity sets continuously evolve. This location capacity is conserved at the individual level (for 85-99% of individuals across datasets), cannot be explained by time constraints alone, and correlates positively with social network size — linking it to the cognitive Dunbar number.
why_it_matters: Resolves the contradiction between mobility stability and continual exploration; identifies the first individual-scale conservation law in human mobility; bridges spatial and social behaviour through the Dunbar-number connection; improves state-of-the-art mobility models with a finite-memory extension.
why_nature: Universal cross-disciplinary finding holding across four independent datasets and collection technologies; connects quantitative mobility to cognitive and social sciences — core NHB territory; multi-year longitudinal scale is methodologically rare; broad downstream relevance for epidemic modelling and urban planning.
added_value_to_my_research: Conserved capacity (~25 locations) as a physics-informed prior constraint in GNN-based mobility flow generation for R2SFCA demand layers
Cross-cultural variation in capacity as an empirical basis for spatially non-stationary 2SFCA catchment radius via MGWR
Capacity trajectory monitoring as a mobility-derived social-isolation risk proxy for mental health accessibility research
Behaviour-derived ~25-slot demand target reframing 15-minute city accessibility as capacity-satisfying supply design
Jaccard-decay turnover modelled as spatiotemporal Hawkes process for synthetic privacy-safe trajectory generation in open-science workflows

figures.caption: Activity set and exploration: individual mobility trace, power-law growth of total visited locations, and example activity set on Copenhagen map
Conserved size of evolving activity sets: average normalized capacity is constant over time; individual gain distributions confirm per-person conservation in 85-99% of individuals
Evolution of activity sets and conservation of time allocation: Jaccard power-law decay shows continual turnover; capacity conserved across all visit-duration classes
Correlation between location capacity and social network size (Pearson r = 0.31-0.54, p < 10^-13) across CNS and MDC datasets
